content:
First note that the given identity (valid for all nonzero real \(x\))
\[
f(x-!1)+f(x)+f(x+1)\;=\;\frac{f(x)^2}{2013\,x}
\]
can be rewritten as the polynomial identity
\[
2013\,x\bigl(f(x-1)+f(x)+f(x+1)\bigr)\;=\;f(x)^2
\]
which then holds for all real \(x\).  In particular, plugging in \(x=0\) gives
\[
0=f(0)^2\quad\Longrightarrow\quad f(0)=0.
\]

Write \(\deg f=d\) and let the leading coefficient be \(a\).  Then
\[
\deg\bigl(2013\,x\,[f(x-1)+f(x)+f(x+1)]\bigr) \;=\;(d)+1,
\]
while
\[
\deg\bigl(f(x)^2\bigr)=2d.
\]
For these to match we need
\[
d+1=2d\quad\Longrightarrow\quad d=1.
\]
Hence \(f\) is linear.  With \(f(0)=0\) we write
\[
f(x)=p\,x,\quad p\neq0.
\]
Then
\[
f(x-1)+f(x)+f(x+1)
  =p[(x-1)+x+(x+1)]=3p\,x,
\]
and
\[
\frac{f(x)^2}{2013\,x}
  =\frac{(p\,x)^2}{2013\,x}
  =\frac{p^2}{2013}\,x.
\]
Equating,
\[
3p\,x=\frac{p^2}{2013}\,x\quad\Longrightarrow\quad3p=\frac{p^2}{2013}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad p=0\text{ or }p=3\cdot2013.
\]
Rejecting the constant solution \(p=0\), we get
\[
p=6039,
\]
so
\[
f(x)=6039\,x\quad\Longrightarrow\quad f(1)=6039.
\]
There is no other nonconstant solution, hence the sum of all possible values of \(f(1)\) is

\[
\boxed{6039}.
\]
